title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
Identify key diagnostic features of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
Apply clinical reasoning to Systemic Lupus Erythematosus management
Recognize common pitfalls in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus

sections.title: Overview of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
sections.content:
**Systemic Lupus Erythematosus**

SLICC criteria, autoantibody profiles, lupus nephritis classification, flare management

**Key Points:**
• Classic: young woman with malar rash, arthritis, serositis, renal/hematologic/neuro involvement
• ANA screening (sensitive but not specific); anti-dsDNA (specific, correlates with nephritis activity)
• Anti-Smith: most specific for SLE; Anti-Ro/La: neonatal lupus, photosensitivity

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Classic: young woman with malar rash, arthritis, serositis, renal/hematologic/neuro involvement
• ANA screening (sensitive but not specific); anti-dsDNA (specific, correlates with nephritis activity)

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• Anti-Smith: most specific for SLE; Anti-Ro/La: neonatal lupus, photosensitivity
• Lupus nephritis: Class III/IV (proliferative) most serious — need biopsy
• Drug-induced lupus: anti-histone antibodies, no renal/CNS involvement (procainamide, hydralazine, isoniazid)

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus:**

• Anti-Smith: most specific for SLE; Anti-Ro/La: neonatal lupus, photosensitivity
• Lupus nephritis: Class III/IV (proliferative) most serious — need biopsy
• Drug-induced lupus: anti-histone antibodies, no renal/CNS involvement (procainamide, hydralazine, isoniazid)

mcqs.question: Which of the following is MOST characteristic of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus?

options.text: Classic: young woman with malar rash, arthritis, serositis, renal/hematologic/neuro involvement
options.explanation: Classic: young woman with malar rash, arthritis, serositis, renal/hematologic/neuro involvement

options.text: Requires invasive testing in all cases

mcqs.question: What is the KEY diagnostic or management consideration for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus?

options.text: Drug-induced lupus: anti-histone antibodies, no renal/CNS involvement (procainamide, hydralazine, isoniazid)
options.explanation: Drug-induced lupus: anti-histone antibodies, no renal/CNS involvement (procainamide, hydralazine, isoniazid)

options.text: Immediate surgical intervention in all cases

mcqs.question: A patient presents with findings consistent with Systemic Lupus Erythematosus. Which finding would you expect?

options.text: ANA screening (sensitive but not specific); anti-dsDNA (specific, correlates with nephritis activity)
options.explanation: ANA screening (sensitive but not specific); anti-dsDNA (specific, correlates with nephritis activity)

mcqs.stem: A 65-year-old woman with new-onset headache, jaw claudication, and ESR of 85 mm/hr is suspected of having giant cell arteritis. What is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.text: Start high-dose prednisone (1 mg/kg/day) immediately, then arrange temporal artery biopsy
options.explanation: Per ACR/EULAR guidelines, GCA with visual symptoms is an emergency. High-dose glucocorticoids must be started IMMEDIATELY to prevent irreversible vision loss. Temporal artery biopsy remains diagnostic even after several weeks of steroid therapy due to persistent inflammatory changes.

options.text: Schedule temporal artery biopsy first to confirm diagnosis before starting treatment
options.explanation: Waiting for biopsy before treatment risks permanent vision loss. Biopsy can be performed within 2 weeks of starting steroids without significantly affecting sensitivity.

options.text: Start low-dose prednisone (10 mg/day) and monitor symptoms for 1 week
options.explanation: Low-dose steroids are insufficient for GCA. The recommended dose is 40-60 mg/day for uncomplicated GCA, or 1 mg/kg/day (up to IV pulse methylprednisolone 1g × 3 days) when visual symptoms are present.

options.text: Order CRP and IL-6 levels before making treatment decisions
options.explanation: While CRP and IL-6 are elevated in GCA, additional testing should not delay treatment in a patient with classic symptoms and elevated ESR. Vision loss can occur within hours.
